questions.question: A pod is stuck in 'CrashLoopBackOff'. Walk through your exact debugging steps.
options: Delete and recreate the pod immediately to reset state
kubectl describe pod → check Events, kubectl logs --previous → check exit code, validate liveness probes, inspect resource limits
Drain the node and reschedule the pod elsewhere
Increase memory limits and redeploy
questions.explanation: Systematic: (1) kubectl describe pod for Events & probe failures, (2) kubectl logs --previous for last crash output, (3) decode exit code — OOMKilled=memory, 137=SIGKILL, 1=app error, (4) check liveness/readiness probe thresholds, (5) inspect resource requests vs limits.

questions.question: Your monorepo test suite takes 50 minutes. How do you cut it to under 10 without dropping coverage?
options: Remove slow integration tests and only run unit tests
Parallelize across agents, use test impact analysis to run only affected tests, cache dependencies and build artifacts aggressively
Run tests asynchronously after deployment and rollback on failure
Split the monorepo into microrepos so tests are smaller
questions.explanation: Test Impact Analysis (TIA) maps test-to-code dependencies — only changed paths trigger related tests. Combine with: parallel agents (pytest-split, jest --shard), layer caching (BuildKit, Nx cache, Bazel remote cache), and artifact reuse across pipeline stages.

questions.question: With 200+ Terraform resources, state drift is increasing. What architectural changes do you make?
options: Run terraform apply -auto-approve on a schedule to correct drift
Split state by domain/environment, use remote backends with locking (S3+DynamoDB), enforce drift detection in CI with terraform plan, adopt Atlantis for PR-driven workflows
Keep one state file but add more detailed resource tags
Migrate everything to CloudFormation for native drift detection
questions.explanation: State decomposition: network/compute/data as separate root modules limits blast radius. Remote state + DynamoDB locking stops concurrent apply corruption. Running terraform plan in CI surfaces drift before incidents. Atlantis enforces plan→apply workflow with audit trail in PRs.

questions.question: Production p99 latency spikes intermittently, affecting ~2% of requests. How do you find root cause?
options: Add more replicas to absorb the load and monitor if spikes decrease
Implement distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry), use exemplars to link high-latency metric data points to specific trace IDs, analyze histograms not averages, correlate with GC/CPU steal/IO wait
Enable verbose logging on all services until the pattern appears
Set up synthetic monitoring to reproduce the spike on demand
questions.explanation: Averages hide tail latency. Histograms expose the p99 shape. Exemplars attach trace IDs to specific metric scrapes — jump from 'spike at 14:32' directly to the offending trace. Correlate with: JVM GC pauses, CPU steal in VMs, kernel scheduling latency, or external dependency timeouts.

questions.question: How do you implement zero-trust networking inside a Kubernetes cluster?
options: Use namespaces to isolate services — Kubernetes RBAC handles everything
Deploy a service mesh (Istio/Linkerd) for mTLS on all east-west traffic, enforce NetworkPolicies, use OPA/Gatekeeper for admission control, bind workloads to IRSA/Workload Identity
Add a WAF in front of the ingress controller
Use Pod Security Policies to restrict container privileges
questions.explanation: Zero trust = verify at every hop. mTLS (service mesh) encrypts and authenticates all service-to-service comms. NetworkPolicies enforce default-deny with explicit allow rules. OPA validates admission (no latest tags, no privileged containers). Workload Identity gives pods scoped cloud credentials — not the overpowered node IAM role.

questions.question: Design a multi-region active-active system with RPO=0 and RTO < 30 seconds.
options: Use a single primary region with cross-region read replicas and promote on failure
Synchronous multi-region replication (CockroachDB/Spanner/DynamoDB Global Tables), stateless app tier, anycast global load balancing, conflict resolution strategy, chaos-engineered failover drills
Schedule hourly backups to a secondary region with automated restore scripts
Use Route53 latency routing with health checks and a 60s TTL
questions.explanation: RPO=0 mandates synchronous replication — async always risks data loss. Active-active requires: globally distributed ACID database, stateless application layer (sessions in Redis Global or JWTs), Global Accelerator or Anycast for <1s traffic rerouting, conflict resolution (LWW, CRDT), and regular GameDays to validate RTO.

questions.question: Why does GitOps prefer pull-based over push-based deployments? What problems does it solve?
options: Pull-based is simply faster because the agent polls on its own schedule

Pull model: agent inside the cluster watches Git and self-reconciles — cluster credentials never leave the cluster, drift is detected and corrected automatically, Git is the audit log, free multi-cluster fan-out
Push-based is more secure because the CI system controls exactly when changes deploy
They're equivalent; the choice depends only on team preference
questions.explanation: Push requires cluster credentials in CI — a significant attack surface. Pull (Argo CD, Flux) inverts this: the agent reconciles continuously. Benefits: no external cluster access, automatic drift correction, full audit trail via Git commits, declarative rollbacks (git revert), and a single operator manages N clusters via different repo paths.

questions.question: A server shows load average of 40 but only 8% CPU utilization. What's happening and how do you diagnose it?
options: The server is overloaded — add more CPU cores
High I/O wait: processes are blocked on disk or network, inflating load average without consuming CPU. Diagnose with iostat -x, iotop, vmstat (wa%), check for NFS stalls or database lock contention
Memory exhaustion is causing swap thrashing
A runaway process is consuming CPU in kernel space, hidden from user-space metrics
questions.explanation: Load average counts running + uninterruptible-sleep (D-state) processes. D-state = blocked on I/O. High load + low CPU = I/O bottleneck. Tools: iostat -x (util%, await ms), iotop (per-process I/O), vmstat 1 (wa column), ps axo stat,pid,comm | grep D. Common culprits: spinning disks, NFS timeout, database lock wait, broken RAID.

questions.question: When would you use a Service Mesh vs an API Gateway, and can they coexist?
options: They're the same thing with different branding — pick one

API Gateway = North-South (ingress): auth, rate limiting, SSL termination for external consumers. Service Mesh = East-West (internal): mTLS, circuit breaking, retries, canary routing, tracing between microservices. They're complementary.
Use API Gateway for REST, Service Mesh for gRPC only
Service Mesh replaces the API Gateway in cloud-native architectures
questions.explanation: Orthogonal concerns, not competing. API Gateway (Kong, AWS APIGW) manages external-facing concerns: consumer auth, rate limiting, API versioning. Service Mesh (Istio, Linkerd) manages internal service communication: mutual TLS, observability without code changes, traffic shaping, fault injection. Production architectures often run both.

questions.question: When would you accept eventual consistency, and when is strong consistency non-negotiable?
options: Always use strong consistency — eventual consistency introduces too many bugs

Eventual consistency (AP): shopping carts, social feeds, DNS, view counts — availability matters more than accuracy. Strong consistency (CP): financial ledgers, inventory counts, seat reservations — correctness is non-negotiable.
Eventual consistency only applies to NoSQL databases
Use eventual consistency in dev, strong consistency in production
questions.explanation: CAP theorem: partition tolerance is a given, so you choose between Availability and Consistency. Real-world nuance: DynamoDB offers tunable consistency per read, Cassandra uses quorum levels (ONE/QUORUM/ALL), CockroachDB offers serializable isolation. Domain matters: losing 1 in 1000 cart updates is tolerable; an overdraft check is not.

questions.question: What's the difference between COPY and ADD in a Dockerfile, and what's best practice?
options: They're identical — Docker deprecated one of them
ADD can fetch remote URLs and auto-extract tar/gzip archives (implicit magic). COPY does one thing: copy local files. Use COPY by default — predictability beats convenience.
COPY is faster, ADD creates smaller image layers
ADD should always be used because it supports more file types
questions.explanation: ADD's 'implicit magic' (URL fetching + auto-extraction) violates least surprise. A .tar.gz added with ADD silently becomes an extracted directory. Best practice: use COPY for predictability; for downloads, use RUN curl | tar xz; for extraction, consider multi-stage builds.

questions.question: What is a canary deployment, and what signals do you use to automate promotion or rollback?
options: A canary deploys to 100% of users but only during off-peak hours

Progressive traffic shift (1%→10%→25%→100%) to the new version. Automate using error rate delta, p99 latency vs baseline, and business KPIs. Tools: Argo Rollouts + Prometheus analysis templates, Flagger.
A blue-green deployment that uses a different naming convention
Canary releases always require manual approval at each traffic step
questions.explanation: Statistical significance matters — don't compare canary vs baseline with too few samples. Argo Rollouts AnalysisTemplate: define max error rate 1%, max p99 200ms, custom Prometheus queries for business metrics. If any metric breaches threshold during the window, automatic rollback triggers.

questions.question: How does Kubernetes scheduling work, and what mechanisms control pod placement?
options: Kubernetes schedules randomly across nodes with available capacity

Scheduler scores nodes via predicates (filter: taints, affinity, resources) then priorities (score: spread, resource fit). Control via: nodeSelector, nodeAffinity, podAffinity/antiAffinity, taints+tolerations, topology spread constraints.
You must manually assign pods to nodes using nodeName
Pod placement is handled entirely by the CNI plugin
questions.explanation: Two-phase: Filter (eliminate nodes that can't run the pod: taints without tolerations, insufficient resources, missing labels) then Score (rank remaining: least-requested, inter-pod affinity weight, topology spread). TopologySpreadConstraints is the modern way to spread pods across zones/nodes with fine-grained skew control.

questions.question: A developer accidentally commits an AWS secret key to a public GitHub repo. What's your incident response?
options: Delete the commit from Git history — GitHub will remove it within hours

Immediately revoke/rotate the credential (assume compromised), audit CloudTrail for usage in last 24h, assess blast radius, check for new IAM entities created, then implement prevention: git-secrets hooks, GitHub secret scanning, Vault or AWS Secrets Manager
Make the repo private to prevent further exposure
Change the secret key and push a new commit to overwrite the history
questions.explanation: Assume the credential was scraped within seconds — bots monitor GitHub in real-time. The 3 Rs: Revoke (disable key immediately), Review (CloudTrail: ListBuckets, AssumeRole, CreateUser in blast window — check for lateral movement), Remediate (rotate related secrets, add detective controls). Git history rewrites don't help — the secret is already in scanner databases.

questions.question: You need to migrate a stateful legacy monolith to Kubernetes with zero downtime. What's your strategy?
options: Containerize the entire monolith and deploy to K8s in one maintenance window

Strangler Fig pattern: route traffic via API gateway, incrementally extract services behind feature flags, StatefulSets with PVCs for stateful components, shadow traffic on new services before cutover, expand-contract DB migrations
Lift-and-shift the VM to a container — it's the same thing with a different runtime
Rewrite everything as microservices first, then migrate
questions.explanation: Strangler Fig lets you migrate incrementally with no big-bang risk. Key tactics: (1) reverse proxy routes specific paths to new services while legacy handles the rest, (2) expand-contract DB migrations, (3) StatefulSets + PVCs for stateful workloads, (4) shadow/dark traffic tests new service before live cutover, (5) feature flags enable instant rollback without redeployment.

questions.question: Which Pod spec correctly configures a liveness probe with an initial delay and defines resource limits?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. It sets initialDelaySeconds (gives the app time to start), periodSeconds, and defines both requests and limits for cpu and memory under resources. Option A is missing initialDelaySeconds and has limits nested under requests instead of as a sibling key.

yamls.label: Option A
yamls.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: my-app:1.0
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8080
      periodSeconds: 10
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "64Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
        limits:
          memory: "128Mi"
          cpu: "500m"

yamls.label: Option B
yamls.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: my-app:1.0
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 15
      periodSeconds: 10
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "64Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
      limits:
        memory: "128Mi"
        cpu: "500m"

questions.question: Which Deployment manifest correctly configures a RollingUpdate strategy that ensures at least 2 pods are always available during a rollout?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct. maxUnavailable: 0 ensures no pods go down before new ones are ready, and maxSurge: 1 allows one extra pod during the update. Option B sets maxUnavailable: 3 which would take down 3 pods at once on a 3-replica deployment — zero availability guaranteed.

yamls.label: Option A
yamls.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: api-server
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 0
      maxSurge: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: api-server
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: api-server
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: api-server:2.1

yamls.label: Option B
yamls.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: api-server
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 3
      maxSurge: 0
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: api-server
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: api-server
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: api-server:2.1

questions.question: Which Service manifest correctly exposes a Deployment internally within the cluster on port 80, forwarding to container port 8080?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. The selector matches the pod label app: web-app, port: 80 is the service port, and targetPort: 8080 maps to the container. Option A uses port: 8080 as the service port (clients would need to call :8080) and is missing the targetPort — so Kubernetes defaults targetPort to 8080 which matches by accident, but the service port is wrong for an internal HTTP service.

yamls.label: Option A
yamls.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-service
spec:
  selector:
    app: web-app
  ports:
  - protocol: TCP
    port: 8080
  type: ClusterIP

yamls.label: Option B
yamls.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-service
spec:
  selector:
    app: web-app
  ports:
  - protocol: TCP
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
  type: ClusterIP

questions.question: This Pod spec has one misconfigured line that will prevent it from being scheduled. Click the wrong line.
questions.explanation: Line with 'cpu: 500m' under limits is fine — the bug is the indentation: 'limits' is nested under 'requests' instead of being a sibling. The correct structure has requests and limits as sibling keys under resources.
questions.yaml:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: backend
  namespace: production
spec:
  containers:
  - name: backend
    image: backend:3.2.1
    ports:
    - containerPort: 3000
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "128Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
        limits:
          memory: "256Mi"
          cpu: "500m"

questions.question: This Deployment has a label mismatch that will cause the Deployment controller to fail. Click the wrong line.
questions.explanation: The selector matchLabels uses 'app: my-api' but the pod template labels set 'app: my-app'. The selector must exactly match the template labels — this mismatch causes the Deployment to fail with a validation error.
questions.yaml:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-api
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: my-api:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080

questions.question: This ConfigMap-based environment variable injection has a bug. Click the wrong line.
questions.explanation: The key 'configMapRef' should be 'configMapKeyRef' when referencing a specific key from a ConfigMap in an env var. Using 'configMapRef' is for envFrom (loading all keys), not for individual env vars with a specific key.
questions.yaml:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: app:1.0
    env:
    - name: DB_HOST
      valueFrom:
        configMapRef:
          name: app-config
          key: database_host

questions.question: This NetworkPolicy intends to allow ingress only from pods with label 'role: frontend' but has a mistake. Click the wrong line.
questions.explanation: 'podSelector' under ingress from should be inside a list item (starting with '-'). Without the dash, it is not a valid list entry, meaning the policy has no valid ingress rules and will block all traffic — the opposite of the intent.
questions.yaml:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-frontend
  namespace: backend
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      role: backend
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    podSelector:
      matchLabels:
        role: frontend
